eyebrow: A free quarter-hour for the real issue
headline: Use the free quarter-hour to name the problem worth solving.
subheadline: Start with the situation, expected result, and consequence; one of them will reveal the question worth carrying forward.
chatStarter: I have several technical problems, but I cannot tell which one is actually worth solving first.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: Naming one worthwhile problem is credible, valuable, and appropriately sized for fifteen minutes.

eyebrow: Know what kind of question you brought
headline: By minute fifteen, separate the symptom from the decision underneath it.
subheadline: Clarify whether you need to fix what happened or decide what should happen next.
chatStarter: I can describe what went wrong, but I suspect a larger decision is hiding underneath it.
proofLine: Free 15-minute fit check. Direct 1:1 support.
whyItWorks: It distinguishes repair from choice, creating immediate clarity without promising a diagnosis.

eyebrow: A goal the first conversation can hold
headline: The opening conversation ends with one goal clear enough to examine.
subheadline: Reduce the ambition to one outcome specific enough to evaluate without designing the full solution.
chatStarter: I know the broad outcome I want, but it is too large to discuss usefully.
proofLine: 10+ years teaching programming. Free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: One examinable goal is a modest, concrete result for an opening conversation.

eyebrow: Find what keeps the question vague
headline: Fifteen free minutes can expose the missing piece in your question.
subheadline: Find the undefined person, condition, or outcome preventing the question from becoming answerable.
chatStarter: The question sounds reasonable, but every answer still feels too generic to use.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: The missing-piece frame promises progress without claiming the entire question will be solved.

eyebrow: Name who the answer must help
headline: Before the free check ends, name who the technical answer must help.
subheadline: Identify the user, teammate, customer, or operator whose behavior defines the problem.
chatStarter: We keep debating the technical answer without agreeing who needs it to work differently.
proofLine: Free 15-minute fit check. Direct 1:1 support.
whyItWorks: Naming the answer’s audience gives a broad technical question an immediate human center.

headline: A quarter-hour is enough to reject the wrong starting point.
subheadline: Rule out the starting point that ignores the goal, constraint, or evidence already available.
chatStarter: I have three possible starting points and worry that one sends the project in the wrong direction.
proofLine: 10+ years teaching programming. Free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: Eliminating a bad start is valuable even before the right plan exists.

headline: Bring three options; the free check identifies the comparison that matters.
subheadline: Compare the options through one shared criterion instead of reviewing every feature of each.
chatStarter: I have three credible options, but every comparison produces a different winner.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: A deciding comparison is smaller and more useful than reviewing every option completely.

headline: Your vague concern leaves the first exchange with one useful boundary.
subheadline: Bound the issue by system, user, consequence, or time so the next conversation has a usable edge.
chatStarter: The concern touches everything, and I need a reasonable boundary before I can discuss it.
proofLine: Free 15-minute fit check. Direct 1:1 support.
whyItWorks: One boundary gives a vague concern shape without oversimplifying its content.

headline: Leave the free check knowing which detail deserves a longer conversation.
subheadline: Identify the detail that could materially change the answer before expanding the conversation around everything else.
chatStarter: There is too much context for fifteen minutes, so I need to know which detail matters most.
proofLine: 10+ years teaching programming. Free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: It promises selective depth rather than a complete answer in fifteen minutes.

eyebrow: Know what support should not absorb
headline: One honest quarter-hour can name what this support should not do.
subheadline: Clarify which decisions fit monthly guidance and which require implementation, access, or another specialist.
chatStarter: I want to know whether this belongs in mentorship or requires someone to implement it directly.
proofLine: Free 15-minute fit check. Direct 1:1 support.
whyItWorks: The negative boundary builds trust by stating what monthly support should not absorb.

headline: The first fifteen minutes should reduce the problem, not sell the service.
subheadline: The free check earns trust by sharpening the question, even if the monthly relationship is not the answer.
chatStarter: I want the first conversation to improve the question, not become a sales presentation.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: It directly opposes sales pressure and gives the free check an earned purpose.

eyebrow: Know what would change your mind
headline: Before the check ends, know what evidence would change the decision.
subheadline: Name the result capable of confirming, rejecting, or revising your current direction.
chatStarter: We keep debating the decision, but nobody has said what new evidence would change it.
proofLine: 10+ years teaching programming. Free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: A named evidence condition converts uncertainty into a disciplined next check.

eyebrow: Spend the month on the right unknown
headline: The fit check identifies the first uncertainty worth paying to reduce.
subheadline: Choose the unknown whose answer could materially change cost, scope, or direction.
chatStarter: There are many unknowns, but only one or two seem worth spending serious time on.
proofLine: Free 15-minute fit check. Direct 1:1 support.
whyItWorks: It connects the free check to a disciplined reason for continuing into paid support.

headline: Use the free opening to choose: act, test, ask, or wait.
subheadline: Sort the issue by the kind of motion it needs before choosing a tool or a larger plan.
chatStarter: I do not know whether this needs action, more evidence, outside help, or patience.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: Four action modes create a compact output without forcing premature certainty.

headline: Minute fifteen should leave one sentence both sides understand.
subheadline: Compress the situation into one sentence stating what is wrong, what matters, and what remains unknown.
chatStarter: I need us to leave with the same understanding of the problem, even if it is not solved yet.
proofLine: 10+ years teaching programming. Free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: A shared sentence is concrete enough to verify before the call ends.

headline: A fit decision leaves with one shared reason.
subheadline: Both sides should know which part of the problem and working style supports—or prevents—a monthly fit.
chatStarter: I do not want a vague good feeling; I want to know why this format does or does not fit.
proofLine: Free 15-minute fit check. Direct 1:1 support.
whyItWorks: A shared reason makes fit mutual, explicit, and less sales-driven.

headline: Fifteen minutes can reveal whether the blocker is technical or directional.
subheadline: Decide whether the next step is diagnosis, prioritization, or a change in technical direction.
chatStarter: I am blocked, but I cannot tell whether the system is broken or the plan is unclear.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: Three blocker types give the short conversation a precise diagnostic boundary.

headline: Before paying, hear exactly how I frame your problem.
subheadline: Use the free check to see whether my questions, language, and reasoning make the situation clearer for you.
chatStarter: Before committing, I want to hear how you think about the problem I brought.
proofLine: 10+ years teaching programming. Free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: The free check becomes a sample of reasoning rather than a sales promise.

eyebrow: One limit that changes the answer
headline: A small first call can put a name on the real constraint.
subheadline: Identify the limit most capable of changing the recommendation: time, budget, skill, access, or risk.
chatStarter: The answer changes depending on our biggest constraint, but I am not sure which constraint is actually controlling us.
proofLine: Free 15-minute fit check. Direct 1:1 support.
whyItWorks: Naming one controlling constraint is a bounded and decision-relevant outcome.

eyebrow: Reduce the overwhelm without denying it
headline: The opening quarter-hour reduces ‘everything’ to one discussable part.
subheadline: Choose one part that can be stated, tested, or assigned without solving the full situation first.
chatStarter: Everything feels connected, and I cannot find one part small enough to discuss properly.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: Reducing everything to one discussable part makes overwhelm visibly smaller.

headline: Before any plan, agree what ‘better’ would look like.
subheadline: Describe the observable change you want before discussing tools, features, or process.
chatStarter: We want to improve the setup, but nobody has described what should be visibly better.
proofLine: 10+ years teaching programming. Free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: An observable better creates goal clarity without requiring a complete implementation plan.

headline: A free fit check can decide which question deserves the paid month.
subheadline: Rank the open questions by consequence and continuity, then see which one benefits from ongoing context.
chatStarter: I have several important questions, but I do not know which one actually benefits from a month of support.
proofLine: Free 15-minute fit check. Direct 1:1 support.
whyItWorks: It links the free check to a thoughtful monthly priority decision.

eyebrow: See what waiting is already costing
headline: Fifteen minutes should clarify the cost of leaving the question unresolved.
subheadline: Compare the consequence of waiting with the effort required to investigate or decide now.
chatStarter: I keep postponing this question because I cannot tell whether leaving it open is actually costly.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: The unresolved-cost comparison gives a serious question economic context quickly.

headline: The first conversation finds where explanation ends and investigation begins.
subheadline: Identify what can be clarified through reasoning and what needs logs, access, a test, or hands-on work.
chatStarter: I do not know whether talking this through is enough or whether someone needs to inspect the system.
proofLine: 10+ years teaching programming. Free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: This boundary prevents reasoning support from being confused with hands-on diagnosis.

headline: A quarter-hour can expose the assumption holding the whole question together.
subheadline: State the belief that must be true for the question, plan, or estimate to make sense.
chatStarter: I suspect our entire question depends on one assumption nobody has stated.
proofLine: Free 15-minute fit check. Direct 1:1 support.
whyItWorks: One holding assumption gives the larger question a clear point of inspection.

headline: Use the first check to name what can safely wait.
subheadline: Separate harmless unknowns from the one issue that needs attention before work continues.
chatStarter: Too many unknowns are asking for attention, and some of them probably do not matter yet.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: Naming what can wait creates relief without manufacturing an urgent task.

eyebrow: Define what the month must earn
headline: The opening fifteen minutes define what success would mean for the month.
subheadline: Choose one visible decision, capability, or working result that would justify continued support.
chatStarter: Before starting monthly support, I want one clear standard for whether the month was useful.
proofLine: Free 15-minute fit check. Direct 1:1 support.
whyItWorks: A first-month outcome connects the free check to an accountable engagement.

headline: Use fifteen minutes to see whether the question gets sharper here.
subheadline: Judge the fit by whether your language, options, and next question become more precise during the free check.
chatStarter: I want to test whether this conversation makes the problem clearer before committing further.
proofLine: 10+ years teaching programming. Free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: Sharper language is an observable, low-pressure test of working fit.

headline: No polished brief—fifteen minutes can produce the first useful one.
subheadline: Capture the outcome, constraint, open question, and next owner without completing a formal intake.
chatStarter: I cannot prepare a proper brief, but I can explain the situation and answer focused questions.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: A four-part brief is useful, compact, and feasible without intake ceremony.

headline: The opening check gives you language for the next technical conversation.
subheadline: Leave with a sentence you can reuse with your developer, teammate, vendor, or next specialist.
chatStarter: Even if we do not continue, I need better language for the next person I speak with.
proofLine: 10+ years teaching programming. Free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: Reusable language extends the value of fifteen minutes beyond this conversation.
